IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important and timely problem—diagnosing whether LaVi models truly ground language in vision—by constructing FOIL-COCO and three focused tasks that reveal substantial failures of state-of-the-art models relative to humans. These findings are likely to change how researchers evaluate multimodal models (shifting attention toward fine-grained, contrastive diagnostics) and to open concrete follow-up work on dataset design and model inductive biases. Impact is somewhat constrained by methodological simplifications reported (e.g., restricted correction candidate sets, a limited set of baselines and missing statistical uncertainty estimates), so while the contribution will strongly influence experimental practice it is not, by itself, transformative across the broader AI field. | B(4): This work addresses an important gap by offering a precise, controlled diagnostic (FOIL-COCO) and three tasks that directly probe whether LaVi models integrate visual and linguistic information beyond language priors — a practical concern for both VQA and captioning research. The large human–model gap reported (humans near-perfect vs. models near-chance) is likely to change how researchers evaluate multimodal systems and to spur work on fine-grained cross-modal grounding. Some limits (Task 3 restricted candidate set, limited model coverage, and unclear code release) temper immediate reproducibility and adoption, but the dataset design and diagnostic framing open clear and significant directions for follow-up work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—diagnosing whether LaVi models truly ground language in vision—by constructing FOIL-COCO and three focused tasks that reveal substantial failures of state-of-the-art models relative to humans. These findings are likely to change how researchers evaluate multimodal models (shifting attention toward fine-grained, contrastive diagnostics) and to open concrete follow-up work on dataset design and model inductive biases. Impact is somewhat constrained by methodological simplifications reported (e.g., restricted correction candidate sets, a limited set of baselines and missing statistical uncertainty estimates), so while the contribution will strongly influence experimental practice it is not, by itself, transformative across the broader AI field.

reviewer_b.justification: This work addresses an important gap by offering a precise, controlled diagnostic (FOIL-COCO) and three tasks that directly probe whether LaVi models integrate visual and linguistic information beyond language priors — a practical concern for both VQA and captioning research. The large human–model gap reported (humans near-perfect vs. models near-chance) is likely to change how researchers evaluate multimodal systems and to spur work on fine-grained cross-modal grounding. Some limits (Task 3 restricted candidate set, limited model coverage, and unclear code release) temper immediate reproducibility and adoption, but the dataset design and diagnostic framing open clear and significant directions for follow-up work.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clearly new diagnostic perspective—FOIL-COCO and three targeted tasks that isolate a model’s ability to detect a single incorrect content word—which is not a standard MS-COCO / VQA benchmark and represents a meaningful conceptual contribution. It distinguishes itself from prior captioning/VQA datasets by constructing minimal foil edits to test fine-grained cross-modal integration, and compares to several prior LaVi baselines. However, the contribution is primarily a dataset and evaluation paradigm rather than new algorithms, and some experimental restrictions (e.g., constrained correction candidate sets) limit the breadth of the claim; thus the novelty is notable but not a highly significant methodological advance. | B(4): FOIL-COCO presents a clear, practical new diagnostic perspective—single-word ‘foil’ captions and three targeted tasks—that meaningfully probes fine-grained cross-modal integration beyond standard captioning/VQA benchmarks. The paper differentiates itself from prior work by constructing paired correct/foil captions derived from MS-COCO and by evaluating detection and correction tasks, which is a non-trivial and useful extension rather than a trivial recombination of existing datasets. While the idea of adversarial or contrastive examples is not entirely novel, the specific formulation and tasks are notable and likely to inform experts and spur follow-up work (despite some instantiation limits such as the restricted correction vocabulary).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new diagnostic perspective—FOIL-COCO and three targeted tasks that isolate a model’s ability to detect a single incorrect content word—which is not a standard MS-COCO / VQA benchmark and represents a meaningful conceptual contribution. It distinguishes itself from prior captioning/VQA datasets by constructing minimal foil edits to test fine-grained cross-modal integration, and compares to several prior LaVi baselines. However, the contribution is primarily a dataset and evaluation paradigm rather than new algorithms, and some experimental restrictions (e.g., constrained correction candidate sets) limit the breadth of the claim; thus the novelty is notable but not a highly significant methodological advance.

reviewer_b.justification: FOIL-COCO presents a clear, practical new diagnostic perspective—single-word ‘foil’ captions and three targeted tasks—that meaningfully probes fine-grained cross-modal integration beyond standard captioning/VQA benchmarks. The paper differentiates itself from prior work by constructing paired correct/foil captions derived from MS-COCO and by evaluating detection and correction tasks, which is a non-trivial and useful extension rather than a trivial recombination of existing datasets. While the idea of adversarial or contrastive examples is not entirely novel, the specific formulation and tasks are notable and likely to inform experts and spur follow-up work (despite some instantiation limits such as the restricted correction vocabulary).

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper appears well-organized with a logical flow from dataset construction (FOIL-COCO) to diagnostic tasks and experiments, and its main contributions are stated early and clearly (diagnostic tasks and failure modes of LaVi models). However, key presentation gaps weaken clarity for a methodologist reader: evaluation metrics are not specified and hyperparameters/reporting for experiments are missing, which makes the experimental section harder to interpret and reproduce. The writing quality is described as adequate and related work coverage is thorough, but the paper would benefit from more explicit notation/definitions for the three tasks and clearer figures/tables summarizing results and statistical uncertainty. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized and presents its contributions (FOIL-COCO and three diagnostic tasks) early and clearly, with a logical progression from dataset construction to experiments and conclusions. Notation and writing are generally adequate and related work is covered thoroughly, making the narrative easy to follow for LaVi researchers. Presentation suffers minor gaps: important experimental details (evaluation metrics, hyperparameters) and code release are not mentioned in the summary, and some figures/tables/examples could be more explicitly described to aid reproducibility. From a clarity and presentation standpoint these are fixable issues rather than fundamental flaws.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper appears well-organized with a logical flow from dataset construction (FOIL-COCO) to diagnostic tasks and experiments, and its main contributions are stated early and clearly (diagnostic tasks and failure modes of LaVi models). However, key presentation gaps weaken clarity for a methodologist reader: evaluation metrics are not specified and hyperparameters/reporting for experiments are missing, which makes the experimental section harder to interpret and reproduce. The writing quality is described as adequate and related work coverage is thorough, but the paper would benefit from more explicit notation/definitions for the three tasks and clearer figures/tables summarizing results and statistical uncertainty.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well organized and presents its contributions (FOIL-COCO and three diagnostic tasks) early and clearly, with a logical progression from dataset construction to experiments and conclusions. Notation and writing are generally adequate and related work is covered thoroughly, making the narrative easy to follow for LaVi researchers. Presentation suffers minor gaps: important experimental details (evaluation metrics, hyperparameters) and code release are not mentioned in the summary, and some figures/tables/examples could be more explicitly described to aid reproducibility. From a clarity and presentation standpoint these are fixable issues rather than fundamental flaws.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a valuable contribution by introducing a focused diagnostic dataset and showing a clear human–model gap, but they also concur that the experimental suite and reporting are incomplete (missing many hyperparameters/metrics, limited model coverage, constrained Task 3, and no ablations/sensitivity analyses). Given these substantive gaps that weaken the strength of the claims and hinder reproducibility, a middle-ground score is warranted. I therefore assign a 3 to reflect a useful and informative paper that nonetheless requires significant additional experimental detail and analyses to fully support its conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a useful diagnostic dataset and runs multiple tasks showing SOTA LaVi models perform poorly, but the experimental suite is incomplete: only a subset of recent multimodal architectures were evaluated, evaluation metrics/statistical significance and hyperparameters are not reported, and Task 3 is artificially constrained to a candidate set. There are no ablations or sensitivity analyses to probe design choices (e.g. foil-generation parameters or model input variants). Related work appears well covered and the authors acknowledge some limitations, but reproducibility is weak (no code/hyperparameter details) and key analyses needed to firmly support the strong claims are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a well-motivated diagnostic dataset (FOIL-COCO) and three focused tasks that clearly probe fine-grained cross-modal integration, and it evaluates several strong LaVi baselines showing a large human–model gap—supporting the main claims. However, experimental coverage has some gaps: evaluation metrics and many hyperparameters aren’t reported, only a limited set of models are tested, Task 3 is evaluated under a restricted setting, and ablation/sensitivity analyses are lacking. Related work is reported as thorough and the authors discuss some limitations, but reproducibility would be improved by releasing code/data and more experimental detail. Overall the experiments are solid and informative but miss some important reproducibility and analysis components.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear claims and provides convincing experiments showing large human–model gaps on FOIL-COCO diagnostics, demonstrating reliance on language-only cues. However, they also note missing methodological details (evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, code) and a constrained instantiation of Task 3 that weaken reproducibility and the generality of one claim. Given the strong empirical signal but nontrivial technical gaps, a slightly reduced but still positive score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claim — that FOIL-COCO reveals LaVi models fail at fine-grained cross-modal integration — is clearly stated and supported by targeted experiments on three diagnostic tasks using MS-COCO-derived foils. However, the paper provides no mathematical derivations, omits reporting evaluation metrics/statistical significance and uncertainty estimates, and does not report hyperparameters or code, weakening reproducibility and the strength of empirical claims. The experimental methodology is partially sound (several baselines including VQA models and captioning baselines were evaluated) but is limited by a restricted instantiation of Task 3, a modest set of multimodal baselines, and lack of statistical tests. Overall the results plausibly support the conclusions but important methodological gaps and unsupported assumptions reduce my confidence in the technical completeness.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and supports them with experiments showing large human–model gaps on three diagnostic tasks built from FOIL-COCO, which convincingly demonstrate models rely on language-only cues. However, methodological details are incomplete (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters not reported), and Task 3 is artificially constrained to target-word selection which weakens the claim about full correction capability. These are important but minor technical gaps that do not overturn the main experimental conclusions.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper targets a central LaVi problem and provides a well-motivated diagnostic dataset with strong empirical comparisons, making it a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue. Although Reviewer A notes some missing methodological details and deeper analysis that warrant improvement, these are implementation and exposition concerns rather than issues of scope or relevance, so the work remains highly appropriate for the target venues.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a central LaVi diagnostic problem that is highly relevant to top NLP/ML venues: constructing FOIL-COCO and three probing tasks directly target cross-modal integration failures. The experimental setup uses multiple competitive baselines (several captioning and VQA models plus a caption-only MLP), so empirical comparisons are adequate. However, I penalize the submission for lacking a formal problem statement or theoretical framing, missing details on evaluation metrics/statistical significance and hyperparameters, and for relying on a single synthetic foil-construction procedure without deeper analysis of potential dataset artefacts—issues that warrant concern but do not make the work out of scope.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it proposes a clearly motivated diagnostic dataset (FOIL-COCO) and three precise tasks that probe fine-grained visual–language integration, a topic central to the community. The work includes human baselines showing large human–model gaps and evaluates multiple state-of-the-art LaVi models, which aligns with the evaluation-focused standards I prioritize. Minor issues—limited detail on released code/evaluation scripts and a constrained formulation for Task 3—are implementation concerns but do not undermine the paper’s relevance or impact for the venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper evaluates a reasonable set of baselines on the same FOIL-COCO tasks and that comparisons are broadly fair, but they also agree important, stronger multimodal architectures are omitted, Task 3 is artificially constrained, and some experimental details/reproducibility elements are missing. Given these acknowledged strengths but clear gaps in completeness and reproducibility that weaken the comparison, a mid-range score reflecting a useful but incomplete comparative evaluation is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates a mix of classical captioning/VQA models (Deeper LSTM, HieCoAtt, Neuraltalk4) and a caption-only MLP+BoW baseline, so it includes multiple competitive baselines but omits several stronger recent multimodal architectures (e.g. MCB-style models) noted in its limitations. Comparisons appear broadly fair in using the same FOIL-COCO data, but Task 3 is artificially constrained (selection from target words) and not all models were instantiated for every task, which weakens fairness. The authors discuss related work thoroughly, yet the experimental setup and missing stronger recent baselines mean the comparative evaluation is incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates FOIL-COCO using a sensible set of baselines (Deeper LSTM + norm I, HieCoAtt, Neuraltalk4 for captioning, and an MLP+BoW caption-only model) and applies them to the same diagnostic tasks/dataset, which aligns with fair comparison practice. However, important omissions and limitations remain: some multimodal architectures (e.g., compact bilinear pooling variants) were not evaluated, Task 3 is artificially constrained to a small candidate set, and evaluation details/hyperparameters and code release are not reported—these reduce reproducibility and leave minor gaps in the comparison completeness.

main_claims: Propose FOIL-COCO, an extension of MS-COCO that pairs images with correct and 'foil' captions differing by a single mistaken word (a 'foil word').

Introduce three diagnostic tasks (caption classification, foil word detection, foil word correction) to probe whether language-and-vision (LaVi) models truly integrate visual and linguistic information.

Show that state-of-the-art LaVi models perform poorly (close to chance) on these tasks while humans achieve near-perfect performance, indicating language-only cues are insufficient and current models lack fine-grained cross-modal understanding.
_summary.methodology: Create FOIL-COCO by generating foil captions with one incorrect content word based on MS-COCO captions; evaluate state-of-the-art LaVi models on three tasks: (T1) correct vs. foil classification, (T2) foil word detection (two settings: among nouns or among all content words), and (T3) foil word correction (restricted to selecting from target words).
baselines: Deeper LSTM + norm I (Antol et al., 2015 / Lu et al., 2015)
Neuraltalk4 (Fei-Fei, 2015) used for captioning in dataset construction
limitations: For computational reasons, Task 3 (foil word correction) is instantiated by selecting the correct word from the target words rather than the full dataset vocabulary.
Evaluation considers two state-of-the-art VQA models (and some captioning/MLP baselines); not all models (e.g. Multimodal Compact Bilinear Pooling) were evaluated.
_summary.topic_area: Language-and-vision (LaVi): VQA and Image Captioning diagnostics

reviewer_a.name: Theory-focused Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher with a PhD in Machine Learning and a track record of work on formal model evaluation and theoretical guarantees for multimodal learning.
traits: Penalises papers that are primarily empirical/benchmarking without accompanying formal problem statements or theoretical analysis
Requires at least three competitive baselines (including strong recent multimodal models) to be compared in experiments; deducts points if fewer are used
Demands statistical significance testing and quantitative uncertainty estimates for main claims; flags results as weak if absent
Critiques papers that rely on synthetic or narrowly constructed dataset artifacts without in-depth error analysis demonstrating generalisability
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Skeptical and methodical: focuses on formal definitions, rigorous comparisons, statistical validity, and whether results truly support the claimed scientific conclusions.

reviewer_b.name: Dataset & Evaluation Advocate
reviewer_b.background: Principal investigator in vision-and-language tasks with experience building benchmarks and diagnostic datasets used by the community to probe model behaviour.
traits: Rewards clear, well-motivated new diagnostic tasks or datasets that isolate specific model capabilities (prefers practical impact over formal proofs)
Positively biases toward papers that include careful human evaluation baselines and show large human–model gaps
Values reproducibility: awards points if authors release code, data splits, and evaluation scripts
Forgives absence of theoretical analysis when the dataset/task design is precise and the empirical evaluation is thorough and informative
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Constructive and impact-oriented: emphasizes the value of new tasks/datasets that expose meaningful model weaknesses and that are likely to drive follow-up work and improvements.
